title: The Hazard of Ingested Alkaline Disk Batteries in Children
abstract:
Modern disk batteries differ great¬
ly in size and composition and may
contain quantities of mercuric oxide,
silver oxide, manganese dioxide, zinc
oxide,
cadmium
oxide,
or
lithium
hydroxide, along with varying
con¬
centrations of other salts. The mer¬
curic oxide battery is composed pri¬
marily of
a depolarizing mercuric
oxide cathode, an anode of pure amal¬
gamated zinc, and concentrated aque¬
ous potassium hydroxide. Although
present in small total amounts, the
strongly corrosive effects of the con¬
centrated potassium hydroxide
con¬
tained in these batteries has been
well described.3
5
Mercuric ions cause inactivation of
metabolic enzymes and denaturation
of proteins in the cells of the target
organs, primarily the kidneys
and
liver." In addition, after ingestion of
an inorganic mercury solution
con¬
taining mercuric ions, severe GI tract
corrosion
can
occur,
resulting
in
hematemesis, bloody diarrhea,
and
shock secondary to volume loss.79 The
absence of these signs in this patient
may have
been secondary to slow
dissociation of the mercuric oxide, the
relatively small amount contained in
the battery,
or
because
of rapid
removal of the battery and its con¬
tents along with chelation therapy.
The value of D-penicillamine10 and
N-acetyl-D,L-penicillamine"'2 as chela-
tors of mercury has been well estab¬
lished. The prompt institution of che¬
lation therapy in this child may have
been partially responsible
for
the
absence of subsequent renal or hepat¬
ic toxic reactions.
References
1. Barros D'Sa B: Mercury battery ingestion.
Br Med J 1979;1:1218.
2. Reilly DT: Mercury battery ingestion. Br
Med J 1979;1:859.
3. Blatnik DS, Toohill RJ, Lehman RH: Fatal
complication from
an alkaline battery foreign
body in the esophagus. Ann Otol 1977;86:611\x=req-\
615.
4. Shabino CL, Feinberg AN: Esophageal per-
foration secondary to alkaline battery ingestion.
J Am Coll Emerg Phys 1979;8:360-362.
5. Votteler TP: Warning: Ingested disc bat-
teries. Texas Med 1981;77:7.
6. Willis GA, Ho WC: Perforation of Meckel's
diverticulum by an alkaline hearing aid battery.
Can Med Assoc 1982;126:497.
7. Kahn
A, Denis R, Blum
D:
Accidental
ingestion of mercuric sulphate in a 4-year-old
child. Clin Pediatr 1977;16:956-958.
8. Robillard JE, Rames LK, Jensen RL, et al:
Peritoneal dialysis in mercurial diuretic intoxi-
cation. J Pediatr 1976;88:79.
9. Winke CL, Fochtman FW, Bricker JD, et al:
Fatal mercuric chloride ingestion. Clin Toxicol
1981;18:261-266.
10. Snodgrass W, Sullivan JB Jr, Rumack BH,
et al: Mercury poisoning from home gold ore
processing: Use of penicillamine and dimerca-
prol. JAMA 1981;246:1929-1931.
11. Aronow R, Fleischmann LE: Mercury poi-
soning in children: The value of N-acetyl-D,L\x=req-\
penicillamine in
a combined therapeutic ap-
proach. Clin Pediatr 1976;15:936-945.
12. Pieter Kark RA, Poskanzer DC, Bullock
JD, et al: Mercury poisoning and its treatment
with N-acetyl-D,L-penicillamine. N Engl J Med
1971;285:10-16.
The Hazard of Ingested Alkaline Disk Batteries in Children
Theodore P. Votteler, MD; John C. Nash, MD, PhD; Joe C. Rutledge, MD
We have treated eight cases of childhood ingestion of alkaline disk
batteries, one resulting in an esophagotracheal fistula. These batteries are
capable of rapid tissue destruction on contact with moist membranes. We
recommend that packaging include proper warning of this hazard and urge
that retained batteries be promptly removed from the esophagus and
stomach by endoscopy or laparotomy. If the battery has reached the small
intestine, further transit should be monitored carefully by roentgenographic
studies until recovery. Perforation is possible after eight-hour retention at a
specific site.
(JAMA 1983;249:2504-2506)
INGESTION of foreign bodies is com¬
mon in infancy and childhood. After
the development of miniaturized elec¬
tronics,
thousands
of
calculators,
cameras,
games,
computers,
and
watches
are being sold each year,
powered by small disk batteries. More
than 140 ingestions of batteries were
reported to the National Consumer
Product Safety Commission in
one
year
(oral
communication,
April
1982). Details of these cases are not
presently available, unfortunately.
See also pp 2495,
2502, and 2509.
Recommendations as to treatment
for patients who have ingested bat¬
teries
have
varied.12
Attention
in
Europe has been focused on the possi¬
bility
of
heavy
metal
poisoning
resulting from their ingestion.3 The
caustic hazard of these batteries has
been
addressed
in
two
separate
reports in which a fatal outcome was
From the Children's Medical Center (Dr Votteler);
Baylor University Medical Center and W. W. Caruth
Surgical
Research
Center
(Dr
Nash);
and
the
Departments
of
Pathology,
University
of
Texas
Health Science Center and Children's Medical Cen-
ter (Dr Rutledge), Dallas.
Reprint requests to Suite 547, Douglas Plaza,
8226 Douglas, Dallas, TX 75225 (Dr Votteler).
Downloaded From: http://jama.jamanetwork.com/ by a UCSF LIBRARY User  on 06/12/2015
 attributed to the battery lodging in
the esophagus, with subsequent
ne¬
crosis of the esophagus and trachea.4
s
Massive mediastinitis developed after
liquefactive necrosis of the tissues
surrounding the retained battery oc¬
curred.
We report eight cases of disk bat¬
tery ingestion
with
nonfatal
out¬
comes. Severe sequelae in
one
case
(No. 1) prompted experiments to dis¬
cover some of the effects of inserted
batteries. In vivo and in vitro studies
show the rapid release of alkali and
tissue destruction. Our findings
es¬
tablish the need for aggressive treat¬
ment of persons who have ingested
disk batteries, at variance to inges¬
tion of inert objects. Subsequent cases
of ingestion in our institution have
been handled with consideration of
the
severe tissue destruction to be
expected if the battery is allowed to
remain in a specific location.
Report of Cases
Case 1.—A 25-month-old boy was ad¬
mitted to
a local emergency room with
cyanosis, tachypnea,
and dysphagia.
A
chest roentgenogram revealed a disk cam¬
era battery lodged in the proximal portion
of his esophagus, present, according to his
mother, for five days. An endotracheal
tube was inserted, food particles removed
from the trachea, and the battery removed
subsequently
by
endoscopy
while
the
patient was under anesthesia. The battery
was identified as a "Union Carbide EPX
825
1.5 V." A 5-mm tracheoesophageal
fistula was noted. Subsequently, cardiac
arrest caused by anoxia occurred in the
intensive
care unit. After resuscitation
and stabilization, he was transferred to
the Children's Medical Center, Dallas. A
gastrostomy was performed, and endosco¬
py showed a 3-cm tracheoesophageal fistu¬
la with extensive necrosis of the mid-
esophagus and posterior trachea, extend¬
ing 1 cm above the carina to within 2 cm of
the cricoid cartilage. A retropleural thora-
cotomy was performed to débride the esopha¬
gus and mediastinal soft tissues. A sacro-
spinal muscle pedicle graft was created to
extend into the chest, suturing the fascial
surface
to
the
posterior
trachéal
wall.
The distal esophagus
was ligated and
a
proximal
cervical
esophagostomy
per¬
formed. An endotracheal tube was main¬
tained with difficulty at the level of the
carina for ten days. He was discharged on
the 23rd hospital day. Histological examina¬
tion showed the esophagus having coagula¬
tion necrosis with black precipitate. A colon
interposition was performed in Denver to
restore esophageal continuity
when
the
patient was 32 months of age.
Case 2.—A 3-year-old boy was admitted
six hours after ingestion of a disk camera
battery. Treatment with ipecac failed to
retrieve the battery from the stomach.
Gastroscopio removal
was unsuccessful,
necessitating gastrotomy.
Mucosal
ero¬
sions were associated with greenish-black
precipitate. The battery was identified as
a "Union Carbide EPX 825 1.5 V." Follow-
up has revealed no adverse effects.
Case 3.—A 21-month-old male infant
was seen by his mother to swallow a disk
battery from his hearing aid. Six hours
after ingestion, the foreign object
was
removed from the stomach by gastrotomy.
The battery was identified as a "No. 675
Premium, made in U.S.A." Severe gastritis
was accompanied by a black precipitate.
Follow-up studies have revealed no esoph¬
ageal or stomach injury.
Case 4.—A 21/z-year-old boy was seen by
his mother to swallow a calculator battery.
A local poison control center, contacted by
telephone, recommended observation, an¬
ticipating spontaneous
passage through
the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. Roentgeno-
graphic studies showed a disk battery in
the proximal jejunum. Laparotomy with
removal by jejunotomy was performed six
hours after ingestion. The
presence of
black precipitate was noted but no gross
ulcération of the mucosa was seen. The
battery was identified as a "Union Carbide
186."
Case 5.—A 5-year-old boy was admitted
to St Paul Hospital in Dallas two hours
after swallowing a camera battery. Under
general
anesthesia,
the
battery
was
located and removed from the stomach by
endoscopy. There were multiple areas of
superficial erosions and black precipitate
present. The battery was identified as a
"National 213, made in Japan." No subse¬
quent damage to the esophagus or stom¬
ach has been noted.
Case 6.—An 11-month-old female infant
swallowed
a
"used"
calculator battery
after her father had replaced the bat¬
teries.
Roentgenograms
confirmed
the
presence of
a small disk battery in the
stomach. Endoscopie removal was accom¬
plished four hours after ingestion using a
four-pronged
basket
snare
while
the
patient was under general anesthesia. The
battery was identified as a "Varta 4176+
West Germany." Follow-up has revealed
no subsequent damage.
Case 7.—A 7 lA -month-old female infant
ingested
a calculator battery when the
calculator she was playing with fell apart
and the batteries dropped out. The child
was admitted for removal of the battery
while under general anesthesia, six hours
after ingestion. Endoscopie removal with a
basket snare was successful, but the bat¬
tery
was noted
to be adherent to the
gastric wall in a coagulum of mucus and
black precipitate. The battery was identi¬
fied as a "Maxwell +G-12, made in Japan."
Follow-up has shown no subsequent prob¬
lems.
Case 8.—A 2-year-old girl was noted to
have
swallowed
a hearing aid battery
before breakfast. The child was admitted
to the hospital at 1 pm the same day for
observation after the battery was located
in the small bowel by roentgenography. No
enemas were given until noon the follow¬
ing day, when roentgenograms revealed
the battery to be in the splenic flexure of
the colon. Two sodium phosphate enemas
were given and the battery was recovered.
An eroded battery was identified by the
inscription "675 R."
Methods
In Vitro.—A variety of disk batteries
were
purchased
in
a
local
store
and
included
Mallory-Duracell
Alkaline
(PX825), Mallory-Duracell Silver (MS76B),
Mallory-Duracell Mercury (PX13B), Eve-
ready No. 191, Sharp FA-50CF, and Hita¬
chi-Maxwell
SR41W.
These
batteries
ranged in size from 0.5 to 1.5 cm.
The
batteries
were individually
im¬
mersed in normal saline in a ten to 15:1
(vokvol) ratio. After immersion, the pH
was serially measured with a pH meter.
More
than
100
"used"
batteries
were
obtained from a jewelry store, where they
had been collected after battery replace¬
ment. These were immersed in saline and
the serial pH checked with a broad-range
pH paper.
In
Vivo.—New
Mallory-Duracell
bat¬
teries of the silver (1.5 V), mercury (1.35
V), and alkaline types were used in animal
studies. The batteries were placed in the
esophagus, gastric antrum, midileum, and
distal ileum of anesthetized mongrel dogs
via celiotomy and enterotomy. Umbilical
tape ligature prevented the batteries from
migrating down the GI tract. The bat¬
teries were left in place for 1, 2, and 7
hours, after which the implant sites were
resected and placed in formalin. Routine
histological processing
was followed by
examination of tissue sections and grading
of the lesions without knowledge of the
duration of battery placement or type.
Results
In Vitro.—Each new battery reacted
rapidly when placed in saline, produc¬
ing
gas
bubbles
and
considerable
black-brown precipitate. After
im¬
mersion in saline, with an initial pH
of 5.0, an alkalinity greater than pH
10 was observed in all but one bat¬
tery. This battery achieved a final pH
of 10.4 after 2V2 hours. Most of the
batteries produced solutions with
a
pH greater than 12, obscuring the
liquid with precipitate. Three of the
"used" batteries produced a rise of
pH to 8.0, with one reaching 9.0 and

lowing the swallowed object to spon¬

ry tract. The size and shape of these
batteries would suggest that they
could be treated in a similar fashion.
The alkaline contents of these biologi¬
allows delivery of caustic material to
the GI tract without obvious accom¬
panying lesions in the oropharynx.
Disk batteries contain sodium or
ingredient. In vitro studies show an
almost instantaneous release of the
furthermore, show rapid tissue de¬
batteries. These changes histological-
ly mimic the damage seen with alkali
chemical rather than electrical effect
of the battery. The immediate conse¬
quences are severe in the esophagus
when the batteries become lodged and
less severe in the stomach because of
protective mucus, acidity, and fluid
volume. The alkali effect is probably
slower in buffered tissue fluid than in
saline. While our animal studies did
cases demonstrate mucosal erosions
and release of battery contents rap¬
idly after ingestion. Case 4 demon¬
strates that the battery maintains
considerable activity even after pass¬
ing the stomach. The ileum seems to
Willis and Ho6 recently reported a
case of perforation of Meckel's diver¬
hours after ingestion, documenting
clinically that transit through the
acidic environs of the stomach does
not deplete the battery of alkaline.
This is supported by the development
authors recommend serial roentgen-
ography or waiting periods of 24 to 48
hours for the development of perito¬
neal signs before intervention. We
should commence before the develop¬
superficial erosions produced in tran¬
consequence; the more severe damage,
however, occurs after prolonged con¬
tact of the leaking battery at a single
tissue site. The time to produce seri¬
ous injury by alkaline batteries is
much shorter than 24 to 48 hours, and
we would prefer early intervention to
prevent peritonitis or mediastinitis.
Based on our experimental data, we
The degree of medical intervention
ingestions will result in subclinical
tissue damage as the battery moves
through the digestive tract. Detection
of impediment to a normal transit is
the key to prophylactic, aggressive
removal of ingested batteries.7 Post¬
ponement of removal until clinical
signs and symptoms indicating exten¬
sive damage, perforation, or both is
hazardous, especially when early rec¬
dependent only on parental observa¬
clinical experience, and the potential
for complications, we have developed
an approach to treatment. Clearly,
larger-diameter batteries impacted in
the esophagus need prompt removal.
